title: Is NGE in Accord with the Classic Heros Journey
display_title: Is NGE in Accord with the Classic Heros Journey
wikitext:
[]: Is NGE in Accord with the Classic "Hero's Journey"             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Jul 27, 2008 2:42 pm Since someone posted this somewhere else and there's already a topic here that roughly deals with NGE in this manner (Shinji Appreciation page 11), I thought I'd bring it up. Article can be found HERE. [quote="The Hero’s Journey: A Campbellian Look at the Metaphorical Path to Personal Transformation"]By Lynne Milum Copyright © 2003 by Lynne Milum. All rights reserved. What is mythology? Have you ever contemplated what makes a great story? How is it that humankind has generated so many great stories throughout history? Why does the reader feel aligned with the heroes of these stories in such a way that the stories go beyond the printed word and impact our own lives back here in "the real world"? While many people believe that myths are stories that are "untrue" or even "lies," in fact, resonant mythologies are metaphors that provide us with symbols of a life we long to experience. To read a myth literally is to miss its higher truth. Mythological narratives may or may not be based in fact. Myths that are temporal will cease to inspire over time and will die out. The perennial myths, however, are those which persevere and project a timeless truth which point the way to our ultimate destiny. To pursue this destiny is The Hero’s Journey. A Campbellian Analysis of the Hero’s Journey There are three major phases to great stories in mythology, based on Joseph Campbell’s work and documented in The Hero with a Thousand Faces.[1] These phases are Separation, Initiation and Return. Key story elements as presented by Campbell follow. Separation: Separation is the culmination of a person awakening from a world of drudgery and despair to pursue a higher calling. The initial step is a ‘Call to Adventure’ where the heroic figure is made aware of a place beyond the world he has known his whole life.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sun Jul 27, 2008 3:08 pm I think this is interesting and that The Hero's Journey can fit to an extent with Eva; the main difference is of course Eva intentionally doesn't have any resolution with it's ending(s). My only other comment is I think it's interesting just how fitting that goddess/mother image is with how Lilith and Yui are. (Same with Misato being Shinji's guide while Asuka's the temptress.)

[]:             Quote Post by Sharaz Destler » Sun Jul 27, 2008 4:03 pm My thoughts? Let it cook for a while. You might be able to come up with a few more links. And don't worry about a thing, Yojimbo. Things like NGE practically exist to be over-analyzed.

[]:             Quote Post by THE Hal E. Burton 9000 » Sun Jul 27, 2008 5:31 pm in some ways, yeah, there are a few areas that seem to correlate, Shinji could also be remembered as the "reluctant" type of hero, though I think most would see him as more "anti-hero" than hero but for the most part however, the classical/mythical treatment of the basic narrative, the "monomyth", doesn't really fit with Evangelion it's just too "non-Western" in source and execution

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Sun Jul 27, 2008 5:45 pm Anti-hero? Lolwut? The Punisher is an anti-hero, Deadpool is an anti-hero, Deathstroke is an anti-hero. Shinji is just a poor schmuck stuck somewhere no one deserves to be. Not someone running around doing questionable deeds for the greater good.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Jul 27, 2008 5:52 pm Anonymous_Evafan wrote: Anti-hero? Lolwut?

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sun Jul 27, 2008 6:43 pm Hal wrote: Shinji could also be remembered as the "reluctant" type of hero, though I think most would see him as more "anti-hero" than hero.

[]:             Quote Post by Defectron » Sun Jul 27, 2008 7:20 pm The hero may encounter the negative side of woman – ‘Woman as Temptress.’ In this scenario, the hero finds himself occupied with selfish pleasures. The ease with which the hero falls into temptation places the path to enlightenment in peril. While the purified hero will be repulsed by these offerings, the struggling hero must soar beyond the sin and despair, to regain his path.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Sun Jul 27, 2008 8:59 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: and instead possesses character traits that are antithetical to heroism.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Jul 27, 2008 9:16 pm Anonymous_Evafan wrote: No one, and I mean no one, in the real world comes anywhere close to a fictional hero.

[]:             Quote Post by THE Hal E. Burton 9000 » Sun Jul 27, 2008 9:25 pm IMO I think you're missing the point, AEF it's the fact that Shinji is put in the role to be teh "hero" and the fact that he doesn't really rise to the occassion and the fact he is willing to basically "let the world go to hell in a headbasket" solely on the basis that he is "too scared" or whatever it is his feelings and ultimate acts of selfishness in the name of "being a coward" when he is not expected to be is what makes him somewhat comparable to "anti-hero" No one, and I mean no one, in the real world comes anywhere close to a fictional hero.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Sun Jul 27, 2008 9:26 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: Don't get your association with the term confused with its actual meaning.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Jul 27, 2008 9:31 pm Anonymous_Evafan wrote: I really don't care what it's supposed actual meaning is.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Sun Jul 27, 2008 9:38 pm I find the category's flawed in general. It's not as simple as writing something in a book makes it fact. Some people consider Bin Laden a hero while we consider him a heartless murder. Unlike most people in this country I will not claim that my perspective is better than theirs. This is the true nature of the world we'd rather pretend is not the case. Everything is a matter of perspective.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Jul 27, 2008 9:40 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: Anno himself said that the characters in NGE aren't heroes.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Jul 27, 2008 9:45 pm Anonymous_Evafan wrote: I find the category's flawed in general.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Sun Jul 27, 2008 10:05 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: You keep using reality to judge the flaws of a fictional term: Why?

[]:             Quote Post by Vegeta 20XX » Sun Jul 27, 2008 10:25 pm Sailor Star Dust wrote: Leaky wrote: "In fiction, an anti-hero is a protagonist who is lacking the traditional heroic attributes and qualities, and instead possesses character traits that are antithetical to heroism."

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Jul 27, 2008 10:56 pm Vegeta 20XX wrote: He prefers to run away from his problems

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Sun Jul 27, 2008 11:37 pm Shinji does have several heroic deeds under his belt. More than 99.9% of people that age would. He's not a hero, an anti-hero, or a villian. He isn't anything but a normal person trying to find his way through a world that vurtually abandoned him. Trying to place him in any catergory is a gross simplification of him as a whole.
